eli3: Imagine you're a teacher grading essays. After the first pass you notice which students are still struggling (the residuals — the mistakes). You teach a second lesson focused entirely on those mistakes. Then you find who is still struggling and teach a third lesson just for them. You keep doing this until everyone is caught up.

Gradient Boosting works the same way. It trains a sequence of small decision trees, where each new tree is trained to predict the errors (residuals) of all the previous trees combined. Each tree makes a small correction — controlled by a learning rate — and the predictions are summed.

The result is a model that starts simple and iteratively refines itself, achieving very high accuracy on structured/tabular data. It is slower to train than Random Forest but often more accurate because it reduces bias directly rather than just variance.

whenWhy.title: State-of-the-art on tabular data
whenWhy.body: Gradient boosting (via XGBoost, LightGBM, or CatBoost) dominates Kaggle competitions and industry tabular benchmarks. It outperforms Random Forest on most structured datasets by reducing bias through sequential correction rather than just averaging.

whenWhy.title: Flexible loss functions
whenWhy.body: Gradient Boosting works with any differentiable loss function — MSE, log-loss, Huber loss, quantile loss, custom objectives. This flexibility makes it the go-to method for ranking (LambdaMART), survival analysis, count regression, and other non-standard objectives.

whenWhy.title: When you need high accuracy and can afford training time
whenWhy.body: GBM is sequential — trees must be built one after another. Training is slower than Random Forest (which parallelises trivially). Tune carefully: overfitting is a real risk, especially with low shrinkage (learning rate) and deep trees.

whenWhy.title: Interpretable feature interactions
whenWhy.body: Shallow trees (max_depth = 3–6) capture interaction effects up to that depth explicitly. SHAP values for GBM decompose predictions into main effects and interactions, making complex models more interpretable than almost any other method.

deepDiveIntro: Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001) views boosting as a numerical optimisation problem in function space. At each step, it fits a decision tree to the negative gradient of the loss function — which, for squared-error loss, equals the residuals. The prediction is a weighted sum of all trees built so far.

Key hyperparameters: n_estimators (number of trees), learning_rate (shrinkage — typically 0.01–0.1), max_depth (tree depth — typically 3–8), and subsampling rate (stochastic GBM, which reduces variance and speeds training).

deepDive.heading: The Additive Model
deepDive.body: The model is a sum of M shallow trees: F_M(x) = Σₘ₌₁ᴹ γₘ · hₘ(x), where hₘ is a regression tree and γₘ is its weight (learning rate × step size). The key insight: this is forward stagewise additive modelling — at each step, add the function that best reduces the current loss without changing previous trees. [1]

deepDive.heading: Pseudo-Residuals (Gradient of the Loss)
deepDive.body: At step m, compute the pseudo-residual for each training point: rᵢₘ = −[∂L(yᵢ, F(xᵢ))/∂F(xᵢ)]_{F=F_{m-1}}. For L = MSE: rᵢₘ = yᵢ − F_{m-1}(xᵢ) (true residuals). For log-loss: rᵢₘ = yᵢ − p̂ᵢ. Fit tree hₘ to these pseudo-residuals. The tree learns to correct the current ensemble's mistakes. [1]

deepDive.heading: Shrinkage (Learning Rate)
deepDive.body: After fitting hₘ, scale it by η ∈ (0,1] before adding: F_m = F_{m-1} + η·hₘ. Small η means each tree contributes less — more trees are needed but generalisation improves. There is an empirical tradeoff: η·M ≈ constant for optimal performance. Typical: η = 0.05–0.1, M = 500–5000. Always use early stopping to find the optimal M. [1]

deepDive.heading: Stochastic Gradient Boosting
deepDive.body: At each step, randomly subsample a fraction f ∈ (0,1] of the training data before fitting hₘ (subsampling). This both reduces overfitting and speeds up training. Column subsampling (subsample features like Random Forest) further reduces correlation between trees. These tricks are implemented in all major GBM libraries. [1]

deepDive.heading: Early Stopping
deepDive.body: GBM can overfit with too many trees. Monitor validation loss during training and stop when it stops improving (patience = 50–100 rounds). This determines the optimal M without cross-validation. Implemented natively in scikit-learn's GradientBoostingClassifier (n_iter_no_change) and all XGBoost/LightGBM APIs. [1][2]

takeaways: GBM fits trees sequentially, each to the negative gradient (pseudo-residuals) of the loss — correcting previous errors. [1]
For MSE loss, pseudo-residuals equal true residuals: rᵢ = yᵢ − F_{m-1}(xᵢ). [1]
Shrinkage η trades off depth vs number of trees: smaller η = more trees needed, better generalisation. [1]
Always use early stopping — more trees will eventually overfit. [1]
Stochastic subsampling (rows + columns) reduces variance and speeds training, similar to Random Forest. [1]
Max_depth = 3–6 is typical; deeper trees capture higher-order interactions but increase overfitting risk. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: Why does Gradient Boosting overfit but Random Forest rarely does?
edgeCases.a: Random Forest reduces variance by averaging uncorrelated trees — adding more trees always helps (variance → ρσ²). Gradient Boosting reduces bias by sequential correction — each tree adapts to the previous error, and with many trees the model can memorise noise. The sequential nature means overfitting accumulates. Fix: use small learning rate, max_depth ≤ 6, and always early-stop. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the connection between gradient boosting and gradient descent?
edgeCases.a: Both minimise a loss function by taking steps in the direction of the negative gradient. In gradient descent, the 'parameters' are weights w; each step updates w := w − η∇L. In gradient boosting, the 'parameters' are the function values F(xᵢ); each step adds a tree that approximates the negative gradient in function space. Gradient boosting is 'gradient descent in function space'. [1]

edgeCases.q: When should you use GBM over XGBoost or LightGBM?
edgeCases.a: Scikit-learn's GradientBoostingClassifier is slower but reliable for small datasets and custom loss functions. For large datasets, XGBoost and LightGBM are dramatically faster (optimised C++ implementations with approximate split finding). HistGradientBoosting in scikit-learn (sklearn 0.21+) uses a histogram approach similar to LightGBM and handles missing values natively. [2]

edgeCases.q: How does the learning rate interact with n_estimators?
edgeCases.a: There is a well-documented empirical relationship: halving the learning rate and doubling n_estimators often gives similar accuracy but better generalisation. The product η × M approximately controls the 'effective' amount of boosting. For any dataset, tune n_estimators using early stopping, then tune η to balance accuracy and regularisation. [1]

edgeCases.q: Can Gradient Boosting be used for multiclass classification?
edgeCases.a: Yes. The standard approach (K-class case) fits K separate trees per boosting round — one per class — using softmax output and categorical cross-entropy loss. This is K times more expensive than binary classification. LightGBM and XGBoost implement efficient multiclass boosting. [1]

sources.label: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 10
Friedman — Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine (2001)
